prompt: Play this short World 6 range-synthesis recap chain.

steps.prompt: Step 1: Range width, board help, and blocker input still leave the overall picture mixed. Which simple action fits best?
steps.feedback_correct_v1: Correct. A mixed synthesis picture still points to the controlled line.
steps.feedback_incorrect_v1: Incorrect. Start from the full multi-signal read before choosing action.
steps.why_v1: World 6 synthesis should not force aggression when the combined range story stays only medium strength.

steps.prompt: Step 2: Position, range density, and street development now agree on a clean pressure edge. Which simple action fits best?
steps.feedback_correct_v1: Correct. When the whole synthesis picture aligns, the assertive line fits.
steps.feedback_incorrect_v1: Incorrect. Let the aligned multi-signal edge lead the decision.
steps.why_v1: The point of World 6 synthesis is to act more strongly only when several range signals point the same way.

steps.prompt: Step 3: One signal still looks tempting, but the rest of the range picture stays too thin to keep pushing. Which simple action fits best now?
steps.feedback_correct_v1: Correct. A thin full-picture read should still release even when one cue looks attractive.
steps.feedback_incorrect_v1: Incorrect. This final recap step is about trusting the whole range picture over one seductive detail.
steps.recap_v1: World 6 synthesis recap should now feel coherent: call when the range picture is mixed, raise when the signals align cleanly, and fold when one attractive cue cannot rescue a thin overall read.
steps.why_v1: The reusable World 6 capstone habit is to synthesize the full range picture before falling back on any single signal.
